content.full_text:
MIH’[\
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
October 23,
1995
Andrew J. Junius
Reuben, Weintraub
& Cera
655 Montgomery St.,
16th Floor
San Francisco,
CA
94111
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—95-284
Dear Mr. Junius:
You have requested advice on behalf of State Senator Quentin
Kopp concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act
(the “Act”).
1
!
Your letter provides insufficient facts upon
whici’ we can makc a deteinination.
Therefore,
we must treat your
letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c)
2/
QUESTION
Is the Quentin and Nara Kopp Foundation (the “Foundation”)
a
“controlled committee” under Regulation 18217?
CONCLUSION
Regulation 18217 establishes criteria for determining whether
an exempt organization under Section 501 of the Internal Revenue
Code may be considered a controlled committee of a candidate under
the provisions of the Act.
The Foundation’s articles of
11
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
v
.nn
c


Andrew J. Junius
Our File No.
1-95—284
Page2
incorporation do not address whether the Foundation will receive
or expend funds for the purpose of attempting to influence the
action of the voters for or against the qualification or passage
of ballot measures.
Therefore,
we are unable to conclude that the
Foundation is not a controlled committee under Regulation
18217 (a) (2).
FACTS
Th Foundation is a nonprofit public benefit corporation
formed on December 29,
1994, to provide financial and other
assistance to charitable organizations.
The Foundation is exempt
from federal income tax under Section 501(c)(3)of the Internal
Revenue Code and from state franchise or income tax under Section
23701d of the California Revenue and Taxation Code.
Senator Kopp is one of five directors of the corporation.
The other directors include Mara Kopp, Kenneth Cera, Richard Leib,
and Peter Bogardus.
Article V of the Foundation’s articles of
incorporation states:
The corporation is organized and operated
exclusively for charitable purposes within the
meaning of section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal
Revenue Code.
Notwithstanding any other provision of these
articles or the bylaws, the corporation shall not
carry on any other activities not permitted to be
carried on (i)
by a corporation exempt from federal
income tax under Section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal
Revenue Code,
or (ii)
by a corporation
contributions to which are deductible under Section
170(c) (2)
of the Internal Revenue Code.
No substantial part of the activities of the
corporation shall consist of carrying on
propaganda, or otherwise attempting tà influence
legislation,
and the corporation shall not
participate or intervene in any political campaign
(including publishing or distribution of
statements)
on behalf of any candidate for public
office.
ANALYSIS
General Rule
The Act requires the disclosure of campaign activities of
candidates and elected officeholders,
including contributions
received and expenditures made.
(Section 84200,
g.)
Generally,
a payment received by or made at the behest of a


Andrew J. Junius
Our File No.
1-95—284
Page
3
candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that the payment was not made for
political purposes.
(Section 82015; Regulation 18215.)
Thus,
payments received by any organization controlled by a candidate
could be considered contributions.
When contributions totaling
$1,000 or more are received in a calendar year, the organization
would qualify as a “committee.”
(Section 82013(a).)
“Controlled committee” means a committee which
is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate
or which acts jointly with a candidate,
controlled committee
...
in connection with the
making of expenditures.
A candidate
... controls a
committee if he, his agent or any other committee
he controls has significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee.
Section 82016.
Regulation 18217 provides an alternate definition of
“controlled committee” applicable to organizations which are
characterized as exempt organizations under Section 501 of the
Internal Revenue Code, provided they are not formed or exist
primarily for political purposes.
(Regulation 18217(f).)
Regulation
18217 provides
1
ii’ pertinent part:
(a)
A nonprofit organization,
as defined in
subdivision (f), shall be considered a controlled
committee, if both of the following apply:
(1)
A candidate, his or her agent,
or
any committee he or she controls, exercises
significant influence over the actions and
decisions of the organization,
or acts jointly
with the organization in connection with the
making of expenditures.
(2)
The organization qualifies as a com
mittee under Government Code Section 82013 (a),
and the organization is operated for political
purposes.
For purposes of this regulation,
an
organization is “operated for political
purposes” if either of the following applies:
(A)
The organization receives or
áxpends funds for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate
or the qualification or passage of any
measure.


Andrew J. Junius
Our File No.
1-95—284
Page
4
(B)
The organization makes
contributions to candidates or their
controlled committees
Your letter states that Senator Kopp does “exercise
significant influence” over the Foundation as described in
Regulation 18217 (a) (1).
Therefore, your question concerns whether
subdivision
(a) (2)
of the regulation is met.
The purpose of subdivision
(a) (2)
of Regulation 18217 is to
avoid classifying a nonpolitical nonprofit organization as a
controlled committee merely because donations are received at the
behest of a candidate.
The articles of incorporation state that
the Foundation will not participate in any political campaign on
behalf of a candidate.
However, they do not preclude activities
in connection with ballot measures.
Therefore,
we cannot conclude
at this time that the Foundation will not be operated for
political purposes as defined in subdivision
(a) (2).
However, if the Foundation receives or expends no funds for
the purpose of supporting or opposing candidates or ballot
measures and makes no contributions to candidates or their
controlled committees, it will not be deemed a controlled
committee for purposes of the Act.
Presumption
Regulation 18217 also provides a “safe harbor” which a
candidate may use in setting up an organization as a nonprofit
corporation without the corporation becoming a controlled
committee of the candidate.
The following criteria must be met:
(c)
For purposes of subdivision
(a) (2),
a
nonprofit organization which is tax—exempt under
Section 501 of the Internal Revenue Code,
and which
is not an organization described in Section 527 of
the Internal Revenue Code, is presumed not to be
operated for political purposes if the organization
complies with all of the factors set forth below:
(1)
The organization does not make
contributions to candidates.
(2)
The name of the organization does
not include the name of the candidate.
For
purposes of this subdivision
(c) (2)
the term
“name of the candidate” means the candidate’s
first and last name or some other unambiguous
reference to the candidate.


Andrew J. Junius
-
Our File No.
1-95-284
Page 5
(3)
The organization does not spend
funds in excess of the amount permitted under
Section 501(h)
of the Internal Revenue Code to
influence or attempt to influence legislative
action.
(4)
The organization does not spend
funds to influence or attempt to influence the
gulifictior or passage of any measure in ar
amount sufficient to gualify the organization
as a committee under Section 82013 of the
Government Code.
(5)
This subdivision
(c) shall not be
construed to prevent the organization from
forming a separate and independent political
committee which is not controlled by any
person described in subdivision
(b) (1) (A) -(C).
Regulation 18217(c).
(Emphasis added.)
The Foundation clearly does not meet the criteria of
subdivision
(c) at this time.
However, it may be helpful to you
in structuring the Foundation’s activities.
If you have any questions concerning this letter, please
contact me at
(916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
